questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using semantic HTML elements instead of generic elements like div or span?
options: Improves accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability
Reduces file size significantly
Automatically applies better default styles
Improves JavaScript execution speed
questions.correctAnswer: Improves accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability
questions.explanation: Semantic elements such as <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, and <footer> provide meaning to the document structure. This helps screen readers understand the page, improves search engine indexing, and makes the code easier for developers to read and maintain.

questions.question: Which description best explains the difference between inline, block, and inline-block elements?
options: Inline elements can have width and height, while block elements cannot
Block elements start on a new line, inline elements flow with text, and inline-block allows sizing while staying inline
Inline-block elements are deprecated and should not be used
There is no meaningful difference between them in modern CSS
questions.correctAnswer: Block elements start on a new line, inline elements flow with text, and inline-block allows sizing while staying inline
questions.explanation: Block elements take up the full available width and begin on a new line. Inline elements flow within surrounding text and do not accept width or height in the same way. Inline-block elements remain inline with text but can be sized with width and height.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes how CSS positioning works?
options: Relative positioning moves an element relative to its parent, while absolute is relative to the viewport
Relative keeps normal flow, absolute positions relative to the nearest positioned ancestor, fixed stays relative to viewport, and sticky switches between relative and fixed
Absolute positioning always places elements on top of others and fixed elements scroll normally
Sticky positioning is no longer supported in modern browsers
questions.correctAnswer: Relative keeps normal flow, absolute positions relative to the nearest positioned ancestor, fixed stays relative to viewport, and sticky switches between relative and fixed
questions.explanation: Position: relative keeps the element in the normal document flow while allowing offsets. Position: absolute removes the element from normal flow and positions it relative to the nearest ancestor with a non-static position. Position: fixed stays attached to the viewport, and position: sticky behaves relatively until a scroll threshold is reached, then acts like fixed positioning.

questions.question: When should ARIA attributes be used in a web application?
options: As a replacement for all semantic HTML elements
Only when native HTML elements do not provide sufficient accessibility
To improve CSS styling capabilities
To define responsive breakpoints in HTML
questions.correctAnswer: Only when native HTML elements do not provide sufficient accessibility
questions.explanation: Native semantic HTML elements already provide accessibility information to browsers and assistive technologies. ARIA should be used only when native elements cannot express the required behavior or state, such as with custom widgets.

questions.question: Why are relative units like rem, em, and percentages often preferred over pixels in CSS?
options: They scale based on context, improving responsiveness and accessibility
They are processed faster by browsers
They are required for flexbox layouts
They prevent layout shifts entirely
questions.correctAnswer: They scale based on context, improving responsiveness and accessibility
questions.explanation: Relative units adapt to the user’s font size settings, parent element sizes, or viewport dimensions. This makes layouts more responsive across devices and allows users to zoom or adjust text size more effectively, improving accessibility.

questions.question: Which approach to applying CSS is generally preferred in production environments?
options: Inline styles for faster rendering
Internal stylesheets for better organization
External stylesheets for maintainability and caching
Using only !important rules to enforce consistency
questions.correctAnswer: External stylesheets for maintainability and caching
questions.explanation: External stylesheets separate presentation from content, making styles easier to maintain across multiple pages. Browsers can also cache external CSS files, reducing load times for returning visitors.

questions.question: Why is overusing !important considered a bad practice in CSS?
options: It prevents CSS from loading properly
It breaks the cascade and makes styles difficult to maintain and debug
It reduces browser compatibility
It disables media queries
questions.correctAnswer: It breaks the cascade and makes styles difficult to maintain and debug
questions.explanation: The CSS cascade is designed to resolve conflicts through specificity and source order. Overusing !important overrides that system, making it harder to predict which styles will apply and increasing maintenance and debugging complexity.

questions.question: How do viewport units like vh and vw differ from percentages in CSS?
options: Viewport units are based on the browser window size, while percentages depend on the parent element
Percentages are based on the viewport, while vh and vw are fixed values
They behave identically in all layout contexts
Viewport units only apply to font sizes
questions.correctAnswer: Viewport units are based on the browser window size, while percentages depend on the parent element
questions.explanation: Units such as 50vw and 50vh are calculated from the viewport width and height. Percentages are usually calculated relative to the size of the parent element, so the two unit types can produce very different results depending on the layout context.

questions.question: What is the key difference between content-box and border-box in the CSS box model?
options: Content-box includes padding and border in the width, while border-box does not
Border-box includes content, padding, and border within the declared width, while content-box does not
Content-box is only used in flexbox layouts
Border-box is deprecated in modern CSS
questions.correctAnswer: Border-box includes content, padding, and border within the declared width, while content-box does not
questions.explanation: With content-box (the default), the declared width applies only to the content area, and padding and border are added on top. With border-box, the declared width includes the content, padding, and border, which makes layout calculations more predictable.

questions.question: Why is maintaining a proper heading hierarchy (h1 through h6) important in HTML?
options: It only affects visual styling of text
It improves accessibility, SEO, and provides meaningful document structure
It is required for CSS grid layouts to function
It ensures JavaScript event listeners work correctly
questions.correctAnswer: It improves accessibility, SEO, and provides meaningful document structure
questions.explanation: Headings create a logical outline of the page. Screen reader users often navigate by headings, and search engines use them to understand content structure. A proper hierarchy makes the document easier to navigate and interpret for both users and automated tools.
